title: Making a leather luggage tag
subtitle: Punch, bevel, and finish a simple two-piece veg-tan leather luggage tag
excerpt: How to make a simple veg-tan leather luggage tag with a loop connector and a window cutout for a paper label. Covers cutting, punching the window, bevelling, burnishing, punching the strap loop, and finishing with a leather dressing.
sourceNotes: The Leatherworking Handbook, Valerie Michael (2010). Tandy Leather project guides (publicly available reference). City and Guilds Level 2 Leatherwork.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rectangular slot cut through the front face of a two-layer luggage tag to reveal a paper card or label sandwiched between the layers. The slot is cut after the outer corners are rounded, using the leatherwork knife against a metal straight-edge. The paper card slides in through the open back or through the slot, depending on the construction method.

content.text: A leather luggage tag is one of the simplest leatherwork projects. It needs a leatherwork knife, a hole punch, an edge beveller, a burnisher, and a short piece of thin veg-tan leather. The
and rounded corners are the two skills the project teaches. Both apply to other leatherwork.

1 to 1.5 mm veg-tan leather (a quarter hide offcut is ample for dozens of tags). A leatherwork knife and a cutting mat. A corner-rounder punch (10 mm radius). A single-hole punch (5 mm or 6 mm, for the strap loop). An edge beveller (size 1 or 2). A wooden leather burnisher. A small piece of clear plastic sheet (acetate or credit-card weight PVC) for the label window, if making a clear-window version. Leather dressing for the final finish. A short length of leather lace or a split ring for attaching to the bag.

Mark the tag dimensions: 90 mm x 55 mm is a standard luggage tag size. Cut with the leatherwork knife against a metal straight-edge in one confident pass per cut. Avoid sawing back and forth, which leaves a jagged edge. Round all four corners with the corner-rounder punch: position the punch at each corner and strike firmly with the hammer.

Mark the label window: 60 mm x 35 mm, centred on the tag face with 15 mm margins on all sides. Score the four sides of the window with the leatherwork knife against the straight-edge. At each corner, make a second cut at 45 degrees into the corner to free the waste piece cleanly; cutting straight to the corner leaves a torn stub. Punch a small hole at each inner corner (2 to 3 mm diameter) before cutting to give a clean stop and prevent the cut from tearing beyond the corner. Lift out the waste piece with the knife tip.
Step 3, punch the strap hole and bevel edges

Punch a 6 mm hole at one short end of the tag, centred and 8 mm from the edge; this is where the leather lace or split ring attaches. Run the edge beveller around the full perimeter of the tag, the window opening, and around the strap hole on both faces of the leather.

Dampen the edges lightly with water and burnish all cut edges with the wooden burnisher until smooth and slightly darkened. Apply a thin coat of leather dressing to both faces with a soft cloth, rubbing in circular motions. Allow to penetrate for 5 minutes, then buff with a clean cloth. Thread a short leather lace through the strap hole and tie with a square knot, or fit a split ring. Write the name and contact detail on a card cut to size and slide it through the window.

attrs.symptom: Window corners are torn rather than clean
attrs.cause: Knife cut at the corner overshot the marked line.
attrs.fix: Punch a 2 mm hole at each corner mark before cutting — the hole acts as a stop for the cut. On new work, always punch corners first.

attrs.symptom: Edge will not burnish to a smooth finish
attrs.cause: Leather is chrome-tanned rather than veg-tanned. Chrome-tan does not burnish.
attrs.fix: Chrome-tanned leather edges must be finished with edge paint or edge coat rather than a burnisher. Veg-tan is the correct leather for burnishing projects.
